content.text: Platform Product Roadmap and Backlog Management
A Claude platform team that operates without an explicit product roadmap is really just a shared ticket queue. Platform-as-a-product requires treating the developer platform as a product with its own
(accelerated Claude adoption with high quality and low friction), and its own

that balances short-term friction removal against medium-term capability investment and long-term strategic bets. The roadmap is the primary tool for communicating platform direction to stakeholders and for aligning the platform team's sprint-to-sprint work with the outcomes it is trying to drive.

attrs.alt: Three-horizon roadmap: H1 committed sprint work, H2 planned features, H3 strategic bets

content.text: The platform roadmap is organised into three horizons with different levels of commitment, specificity, and mutability. Items move from H3 to H2 to H1 as they are validated, specified, and scheduled.
Friction backlog P0/P1, bug queue, SLA obligations
Survey themes, TTFD analysis, strategic asks
H3 items should never be presented to consuming teams as commitments. They are hypotheses about where the platform needs to go, validated through discovery work before being promoted to H2.
A platform roadmap item starts as a signal (friction log entry, survey theme, or strategic ask) and ends as a shipped feature with a measured outcome. The lifecycle has five stages.
Signal: a friction item, survey theme, or leadership ask is captured and triaged.
Discovery: the platform team validates the problem scope, estimates impact, and sketches a solution. For H2 items this takes 1–2 weeks of investigation.
Specification: the item is fully specified with acceptance criteria, API design (if applicable), and an effort estimate. Moves from H2 to H1-ready.
Delivery: the item is scheduled in a sprint and shipped.
Measurement: TTFD, NPS, or friction log impact is measured 30–60 days after shipping. The item is closed only when the outcome is confirmed.

## Roadmap Item Template

### [ITEM-42] Self-Service Eval Runner CLI

**Horizon:** H2 → H1 (targeting Q3 sprint 2)
**Source signal:** DevEx survey Q1 2025 — "eval runner hard to configure locally" (18 mentions)
**Priority score:** 14.0 (P1)

**Problem Statement:**
Teams need to run the eval regression suite locally before opening a PR.
Currently, this requires manually configuring 4 environment variables and
understanding the evals/ directory layout. Average setup time: 3 hours.

**Proposed Solution:**
`acme-eval` CLI with guided setup:
```
acme-eval init               # detects project, sets env vars
acme-eval run                # runs suite against local code
acme-eval run --baseline main  # compares with main branch
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] `acme-eval init` completes in under 2 minutes on a fresh clone
- [ ] `acme-eval run` runs the full suite and outputs a pass/fail summary
- [ ] Documentation covers installation and first run
- [ ] Starter template updated to include acme-eval in dev dependencies

**Effort Estimate:** 8 engineer-days
**Owner:** @bob (TC, eval module)
**Target Sprint:** Q3-S2

**Success Metric:** t₃ (dev-to-staging) for teams using CLI drops by ≥ 1 day
**Measurement Date:** 60 days after release

The platform backlog has three layers that serve different purposes and are reviewed at different cadences. Conflating them leads to a backlog that is simultaneously too large to prioritize and too small to plan from.

The now backlog contains all items scheduled for the current and next sprint. Items here are fully specified, estimated, and assigned. This layer is managed in the team's sprint board (Linear or Jira) and reviewed in daily standup.

The refined backlog contains items that have been discovered and specified but not yet scheduled. Each item has an effort estimate and acceptance criteria. Items are pulled into the now backlog during sprint planning based on their priority score and available capacity.

The idea backlog is a holding area for signals that have not yet been through discovery. Items here may be friction log entries, survey themes, or strategic asks. They have no estimate and no acceptance criteria. The platform team reviews this layer monthly to select items for discovery work.

# backlog_item_states.yml  — state machine for platform backlog items
states:
  idea:
    description: "Raw signal, no discovery done"
    backlog_layer: idea
    next_states: [discovering, wont_fix]

  discovering:
    description: "Platform team validating scope and solution"
    backlog_layer: idea
    next_states: [ready_for_refinement, wont_fix]

  ready_for_refinement:
    description: "Problem validated; needs full spec and estimate"
    backlog_layer: refined
    next_states: [refined]

  refined:
    description: "Fully specified; ready to schedule"
    backlog_layer: refined
    next_states: [in_sprint]

  in_sprint:
    description: "Scheduled in current or next sprint"
    backlog_layer: now
    next_states: [shipped]

  shipped:
    description: "Released; awaiting outcome measurement"
    backlog_layer: now
    next_states: [closed]

  closed:
    description: "Outcome measured; item complete"

  wont_fix:
    description: "Declined with documented rationale"

The roadmap is only useful if consuming teams can see it and trust it. The platform team publishes the roadmap in the IDP on the first Monday of each quarter and holds a 45-minute roadmap review with team leads — not to negotiate items, but to explain the prioritisation reasoning and collect input for the next cycle.
H1 items (committed) and H2 items (planned) are visible to all engineers. H3 items are marked 'directional' with an explicit caveat that they may not ship.
45 minutes with engineering team leads. Format: 5 min recap of what shipped last quarter, 20 min H1/H2 walkthrough, 20 min Q&A.
one-paragraph summary of what moved from H2 to H1, what was added to H3, and what shipped. Sent to a platform-updates mailing list.
any item that moves from H2 to 'deferred indefinitely' must be communicated directly to the teams that requested it before the next roadmap review.
The hardest prioritisation challenge for a platform team is balancing
(building what will enable new use cases tomorrow). Both are necessary; neither should dominate indefinitely. The allocation model ties the balance to the platform's maturity stage.
Onboarding friction is the primary adoption blocker
Core friction resolved; capability gaps now limit expansion
Stable base; invest in capabilities that unlock next-order use cases
Stop the bleed; no new capabilities until base is stable

The 20% standing allocation for friction items (from the friction tracking system) is the floor, not the ceiling. At the early platform stage, friction work should dominate. Failing to remove onboarding friction in the first 6 months of a platform's life is the single most common cause of internal platform abandonment.

Platform roadmap items should trace to quarterly OKRs. Without this link, the platform team cannot justify its roadmap to engineering leadership, and individual engineers cannot see how their sprint work connects to the outcomes the organisation cares about.

## Platform Team OKRs — Q3 2025

### Objective: Reduce time-to-first-Claude-deploy to ≤ 5 days for 80% of new teams

**KR1:** p50 TTFD (staging) ≤ 3 days (from 6 days in Q2)
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-42 (eval CLI), ITEM-38 (CI template update)

**KR2:** p90 TTFD (staging) ≤ 7 days (from 14 days in Q2)
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-39 (proactive outreach automation)

**KR3:** Onboarding CSAT ≥ 4.0 / 5 (from 3.8 in Q2 survey)
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-41 (starter template auth preconfiguration)

### Objective: Platform health score ≥ 85 / 100

**KR1:** SDK test coverage ≥ 90% across all modules
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-44 (retry module test gap), ITEM-45 (prompts module test gap)

**KR2:** Zero P0 security findings in quarterly Semgrep scan
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-43 (update bandit rules), dependency upgrade sprint

**KR3:** All CODEOWNERS modules have ≥ 2 active trusted committers
  → Roadmap items: ITEM-46 (Q3 TC nomination cycle)
Platform sprint planning differs from product sprint planning in one critical way: platform teams have

— planned roadmap work and reactive support and on-call work — competing for the same capacity. Failing to account for reactive demand leads to sprints that are perpetually over-committed and roadmap items that slip every quarter.

## Sprint Capacity Model — Platform Team (4 engineers, 2-week sprint)

Total capacity: 4 engineers × 9 working days × 80% focus = 28.8 engineer-days
(20% buffer for meetings, code review, and context-switching)

Allocation:
| Category                     | % of capacity | Days |
|------------------------------|---------------|------|
| Roadmap features (H1/H2)     | 40%           | 11.5 |
| Friction items (P0/P1 backlog)| 20%          | 5.8  |
| On-call + support            | 25%           | 7.2  |
| Tech debt + testing          | 15%           | 4.3  |
| Total                        | 100%          | 28.8 |

On-call engineer rotates weekly. That engineer's capacity is reserved
entirely for support; their roadmap allocation drops to 0 for that week.

Platform teams are frequently interrupted by urgent requests: a production regression in the SDK, a security vulnerability in a dependency, or a critical team blocked on provisioning. The sprint planning model must account for this explicitly rather than treating it as 'extra' work.
P-Incident (production impact): drop everything; on-call engineer handles with backup from whoever is closest to the affected module.
Urgent friction (team blocked, can't ship): on-call engineer handles within 1 business day. If it exceeds 2 days of effort, it becomes a sprint item and displaces the lowest-priority roadmap item.
Non-urgent requests: logged in the friction backlog; triaged at next weekly review. No immediate action.

The on-call engineer is not a catch-all for all platform questions. Engineers with non-urgent questions should consult the IDP documentation and #platform-claude-sdk Slack first. The on-call rotation is for incidents and blocked teams, not for general consultation.
H3 items graduate to H2 only after discovery work validates that the problem is real, the scope is understood, and the proposed solution is technically feasible. Discovery work is time-boxed to
for a single item. If 5 days of discovery cannot produce a clear problem definition and solution sketch, the item is returned to H3 with a note about what remains unknown.
one paragraph describing the developer experience gap, with evidence (friction log count, survey mentions, TTFD impact).
a 1–2 page design doc covering the proposed API surface, integration points, and known unknowns. Not a full spec.
rough estimate in engineer-days for the full implementation. ±50% accuracy is acceptable at this stage.
which DevEx metric will improve, and by how much, if this ships?
external dependencies (Anthropic API changes, security review), known technical risks.

## Discovery Brief — ITEM-42: Self-Service Eval Runner CLI

**Problem Statement:**
Teams need to run the eval suite locally before opening a PR. Current
setup requires manually configuring 4 env vars and understanding the
evals/ directory layout. Friction log: 18 mentions in Q1, avg severity 2.0.
TTFD impact: adds ~0.5 days to t₃ for teams that encounter it.

**Solution Sketch:**
`acme-eval` CLI:
- `init`: detects project root, reads .env.example, prompts for missing vars
- `run`: runs evals/regression/run_evals.py with correct env; outputs pass/fail
- `run --baseline main`: downloads main baseline from CI artifact store
- Ships as a separate pip package `acme-eval`; added to starter template dev deps

**Effort Estimate:** 8 engineer-days (±50%)
  - CLI skeleton + init command: 2d
  - run command + output formatter: 2d
  - baseline download from CI artifacts: 2d
  - docs + starter template update: 2d

**Success Metric:** t₃ p50 drops by ≥ 1 day for teams using the CLI

**Risks:**
- CI artifact store access requires IAM role; needs security review (1d)
- Teams using non-standard eval suites may need customisation hooks

**Recommendation:** Promote to H2, target Q3-S2

Platform teams receive roadmap requests from multiple stakeholders with different authority levels and different types of asks. Without an explicit stakeholder management model, the loudest voice wins and the roadmap becomes a collection of political commitments rather than a prioritised set of developer experience investments.
Log in friction backlog; score and prioritise through normal process
Add to H3; validate through discovery before promoting
H2 migration items; communicate to consuming teams
15% allocation in sprint; must have measurable DevEx impact

The Product Owner is the single decision-maker for roadmap prioritisation within the parameters set by engineering leadership. Stakeholders provide input; the PO makes the call. This prevents the roadmap from becoming a committee document where every team's request carries equal weight regardless of developer experience impact.
a backlog with 200+ items has no prioritisation. If everything is in the backlog, nothing is prioritised. Aggressively decline, archive, or merge items so the refined backlog stays under 20 items.
treating H2 items as committed when they are not leads to consuming teams building dependencies on features that slip. Mark H2 items clearly as 'planned, not committed'.
a roadmap with no friction items communicates that the platform team does not take developer pain seriously. Friction items must be visible in the H1 roadmap.
asking all 30 consuming teams to vote on roadmap priority produces a roadmap optimised for political equity, not developer experience impact. Use the priority scoring model, not a vote.

shipping a roadmap item and declaring success without measuring the DevEx outcome is the most common way a platform team loses credibility. Every H1 item must have a measurement date and a defined success metric.

A platform product roadmap organises work across three horizons — committed sprint items (H1), planned features (H2), and strategic bets (H3) — and provides consuming teams with a visible, trustworthy view of where the platform is heading. The backlog has three layers (idea, refined, now) that correspond to three levels of readiness. Roadmap items trace to quarterly OKRs, and the allocation between friction work and capability work is calibrated to the platform's maturity stage. Publishing the roadmap in the IDP, holding quarterly roadmap reviews, and communicating changes proactively are what convert a private sprint board into a platform product that consuming teams can depend on.
Organise the roadmap into H1 (committed), H2 (planned), and H3 (directional) — never present H3 as a commitment.
Route all items through the five-stage lifecycle: signal → discovery → specification → delivery → measurement.
Maintain three backlog layers: idea (unrefined), refined (ready to schedule), and now (in sprint).
Allocate friction vs. capability work by platform maturity stage; the 20% friction floor is always enforced.
Publish the roadmap in the IDP and hold a quarterly review with team leads.
Trace every H1 item to a quarterly OKR so the team can explain why they are building what they are building.
